summary: In this talk, Jürgen Schmidhuber outlines his theory that the universe, and all logically possible universes, are deterministically computed by a short, optimal computer program. He argues against true physical randomness and quantum computing, while proposing a 'computational theology' where every individual's life is an indispensable, computationally necessary part of the grand scheme.

principles.statement: The universe is deterministically computed by a short computer program.
principles.rationale: All electrons behave the same way because they are essentially reusing the same piece of code. A deterministic universe with deterministic observers is fully compatible with all physical observations, including quantum mechanics.
principles.source_quote: Many scientists are now taking seriously the possibility that all of reality is being computed by a short computer program

principles.statement: True randomness does not exist; apparent randomness is pseudo-random.
principles.rationale: True randomness is fundamentally harder to compute than regular, deterministic patterns. Therefore, phenomena like beta decay must be pseudo-random, much like the decimal expansion of pi.
principles.source_quote: Because randomness, random stuff, where everything suddenly dissolves into arbitrariness is fundamentally harder to compute than regular stuff even by the optimal method.

principles.statement: Your life is an indispensable part of the universe's grand scheme.
principles.rationale: Editing your specific life out of the universe's history would require a longer, and therefore slower, program. Because the universe is computed optimally, your interconnected existence is computationally necessary.
principles.source_quote: So your life, from this perspective, is not insignificant. It is an indispensable part of the grand scheme of things.

principles.rationale: Artificial agents can learn to solve complex problems entirely deterministically, without any random aspects in their artificial brains. Human thought processes operate under similar deterministic computational rules.

frameworks.name: The Optimal Method for Computing All Universes
frameworks.when_to_apply: When attempting to mathematically formulate or simulate all logically possible computable universes.
steps: Systematically enumerate all possible programs through a master program.
Allocate run-time to all of these programs in an optimal way.
Ensure that each possible universe's history is computed as quickly as if it were computed by that specific universe's fastest program.
frameworks.source_quote: You essentially, systematically enumerate all possible programs through a program, and then you allocate run-time to all of these programs in an optimal way that makes sure that each possible universe's history is being computed as quickly as if it were computed by this universe's fastest program

mental_models.name: The Universe as a Video Game Simulation
mental_models.description: Viewing physical reality as a high-resolution 3D simulation where physical laws are just rules defined by a series of zeroes and ones.
mental_models.example: Explaining the identical behavior of all electrons by comparing them to a video game reusing the same sub-program or piece of code again and again.

mental_models.name: Computational Theology
mental_models.description: Using computer science and algorithmic complexity to answer deep philosophical and theological questions, such as the significance of human life or the nature of free will.
mental_models.example: Proving that a human life is significant because a universe program that edits out a specific person's life would be longer and less computationally optimal than one that includes it.

heuristics: To predict the future, look for the most regular, non-random outcomes, as they can be computed by the fastest and shortest programs.
To a man with a computer, everything looks like a computation.

signature_quotes.text: To a man with a hammer, everything looks like a nail. To a man with a computer, everything looks like a computation.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why computer scientists view the universe as a simulation.

signature_quotes.text: Quantum computers are sexy, but essentially, they are dead.
signature_quotes.context: Predicting the failure of practical quantum computing due to its consumption of traditional computational power.
Discussing how simulated artificial brains learn and solve problems entirely deterministically.

signature_quotes.text: Your life, from this perspective, is not insignificant. It is an indispensable part of the grand scheme of things.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding his explanation of computational theology and the optimal computation of the universe.

anti_patterns.description: Believing that quantum physics and Bell's inequality prove the universe cannot be deterministic.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: A deterministically-computed universe with deterministically computed observers is totally compatible with all existing physical observations.

anti_patterns.description: Believing in true physical randomness, such as random beta decay.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: True randomness is fundamentally harder to compute than regular stuff. Physical phenomena that appear random are actually pseudo-random, computable by short programs.

anti_patterns.description: Expecting practical success from quantum computing.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It consumes too much traditional computation power, making it inefficient in the context of the optimal method of computing everything.
